---
title: "Chapman's Oak (Quercus chapmanii)"
slug: "quercus-chapmanii"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://static.inaturalist.org/photos/622641920/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Chapman's Oak"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Fagales"
tax_family: "Fagaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Fagales", "Fagaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://static.inaturalist.org/photos/622641920/medium.jpg" alt="Chapman's Oak" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/stevecollins">
stevecollins</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/341967185">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Fagales
-   **Family:** Fagaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=167642)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not available
-   **Rarity note:** Common across Florida.

## Natural history notes

A small, often shrubby oak characterized by its thick, leathery leaves with a pale, sometimes fuzzy underside. In Florida, it is commonly found in dry, sandy habitats such as scrub and sandhills. It produces acorns that serve as an important food source for wildlife, and like other oaks, it is a host plant for a wide variety of insects.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/167642-Quercus-chapmanii)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/644)
